title: Vietnamese Chicken Pho (Pho Ga)

ingredients.name: whole chicken or 800 g chicken pieces with bone

subs.name: 800 g mixed vegetables and tofu with vegetable stock for a vegan pho
subs.notes: use soy sauce instead of fish sauce

ingredients.name: fresh ginger, halved lengthwise
visual_checks: char directly over flame or dry-toast in pan for depth
ingredients.notes: char directly over flame or dry-toast in pan for depth

ingredients.name: onion, halved
visual_checks: charred alongside the ginger for sweetness
ingredients.notes: charred alongside the ginger for sweetness

subs.name: fennel seeds (sombu), 1 tsp, for a milder anise note
subs.notes: the spice profile will be subtler but still aromatic

ingredients.name: flat rice noodles (pho noodles)
visual_checks: soak in cold water 20 minutes, blanch 1 minute in boiling water before serving
ingredients.notes: soak in cold water 20 minutes, blanch 1 minute in boiling water before serving

subs.name: any flat rice noodle or thin vermicelli; even leftover rice works as a rice soup variant
3 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt

ingredients.name: lime or lemon, wedges for serving

steps.text: Char the ginger and halved onion: hold them over a gas flame with tongs, turning, until charred in patches and soft, about 4-5 minutes. Alternatively, place cut-side down in a dry heavy pan over high heat until blackened. This step creates a sweet smokiness that is the signature of pho broth.

Place the chicken in a large pot with 2 litres of cold water. Bring to a boil over high heat. When the scum rises (about 5 minutes), pour off the water and rinse both the chicken and pot. This parboiling removes impurities and produces a clear, clean broth.

Return the cleaned chicken to the pot with fresh cold water (2 litres). Add the charred ginger, charred onion, star anise, cinnamon stick and cloves. Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce to the lowest simmer — the broth should barely murmur, not roll. Simmer for 50-60 minutes until the chicken is completely tender and the broth is fragrant.

Remove the chicken and set aside to cool slightly. Strain the broth through a fine sieve into a clean pot, discarding all solids. Season with fish sauce, sugar and salt. The broth should taste clear, savoury and deeply fragrant. Return to a gentle simmer.
When the chicken is cool enough to handle, shred the meat from the bones using your fingers or two forks. Discard skin and bones. Fluff the shredded meat and set aside.

Prepare the noodles: blanch the soaked rice noodles in a separate pot of boiling water for 1 minute until just tender. Drain and divide between 4 large deep bowls. Top each bowl with shredded chicken.

Ladle the hot broth over the noodles and chicken — it should be piping hot. Garnish with spring onion, coriander sprigs and bean sprouts. Serve immediately with lime wedges and sliced green chillies on the side so each diner can customise their bowl.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → 800 g mixed vegetables and tofu with vegetable stock for a vegan pho (use soy sauce instead of fish sauce); fish-sauce → 3 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt; star-anise → fennel seeds (sombu), 1 tsp, for a milder anise note (the spice profile will be subtler but still aromatic); noodles → any flat rice noodle or thin vermicelli; even leftover rice works as a rice soup variant
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use a large stainless steel patila or pressure cooker (without pressurising it) for the broth — simmer with the lid ajar for the full time. Charring ginger and onion is easiest over an Indian gas flame using tongs. Star anise (annachi mokku) and cinnamon are Indian pantry staples. Flat rice noodles are sold in large Indian supermarkets; if unavailable, thin rice vermicelli (semiya made from rice) works. Fish sauce can be replaced with soy sauce.
